title: Datasets for ArchaeoPhases
text: package allows replicate following results:  lezoux: chronology potter’s kiln Lezoux (France) – Menessier-Jouannet et al. (1995)
code: ## Import ChronoModel output lezoux_path <- system.file("chronomodel/lezoux/Chain_all_Events.csv",                             package = "fasti") lezoux_event <- read_chronomodel_events(lezoux_path, sep = ";", dec = ",")  ## Plot MCMC sample plot(lezoux_event, interval = "hpdi")

title: Datasets for ArchaeoPhases
text: package allows replicate Bosch et al. (2015) results OxCal (ChronoModel).
code: ## Install and load oxcAAR # install.packages("oxcAAR") library(oxcAAR)  ## Download and setup OxCal quickSetupOxcal()  ## Import the OxCal script path_script <- system.file("oxcal/ksarakil.oxcal", package = "fasti") ksarakil_script <- paste0(readLines(path_script), collapse = "\n")  ## Execute OxCal ksarakil_file <- executeOxcalScript(ksarakil_script) ksarakil_text <- readOxcalOutput(ksarakil_file) ksarakil_data <- parseFullOxcalOutput(ksarakil_text)  ## Get the MCMC samples path_mcmc <- paste0(dirname(ksarakil_file), "/MCMC_Sample.csv") mcmc <- read.csv(path_mcmc, header = TRUE, sep = ",", dec = ".")

title: Multivariate Data Analysis
text: Simple Principal Components Analysis (PCA) Correspondence Analysis (CA) based Singular Value Decomposition (SVD). package provides S4 classes methods compute, extract, summarize visualize results multivariate data analysis. also includes methods partial bootstrap validation. many good packages multivariate data analysis (FactoMineR, ade4 ca, extended FactoExtra). dimensio designed simple possible, providing necessary tools explore results analysis.

title: Multivariate Data Analysis
text: can install released version dimensio CRAN : development version GitHub :
code: install.packages("dimensio") # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("tesselle/dimensio")

title: Multivariate Data Analysis
code: ## Load packages library(dimensio)  library(ggplot2) library(ggrepel) library(khroma) ## Load data data(iris)  ## Compute PCA ## (non numeric variables are automatically removed) X <- pca(iris, center = TRUE, scale = TRUE) #> 1 qualitative variable was removed: Species.

title: Multivariate Data Analysis
text: dimesion provides several methods extract results: get_data() returns original data. get_contributions() returns contributions definition principal dimensions. get_coordinates() returns principal coordinates. get_correlations() returns correlations variables dimensions. get_cos2() returns cos2 values (.e. quality representation points factor map). get_eigenvalues() returns eigenvalues, percentages variance cumulative percentages variance.

title: Multivariate Data Analysis
text: dimensio uses ggplot2 plotting informations. Visualization methods produce graphics elements possible: makes easy customize diagrams (e.g. using extra layers, themes scales).
code: ## Plot active individuals by group plot_rows(X, group = iris$Species) +   ggplot2::stat_ellipse() + # Add ellipses   ggplot2::theme_bw() + # Change theme   khroma::scale_colour_highcontrast() # Custom color scale  ## Plot all individuals by cos2 plot_rows(X, highlight = "cos2") +   ggplot2::theme_bw() + # Change theme   ggplot2::scale_size_continuous(range = c(1, 3)) + # Custom size scale   khroma::scale_color_iridescent() # Custom color scale ## Plot variables factor map plot_columns(X) +   ggrepel::geom_label_repel() + # Add repelling labels   ggplot2::theme_bw() # Change theme  ## Highlight contributions plot_columns(X, highlight = "contrib") +   ggrepel::geom_label_repel() + # Add repelling labels   ggplot2::theme_bw() + # Change theme   khroma::scale_color_YlOrBr(range = c(0.5, 1)) # Custom color scale ## Plot eigenvalues plot_variance(X, variance = FALSE, cumulative = FALSE) +   ggplot2::theme_bw() # Change theme  ## Plot percentages of variance plot_variance(X, variance = TRUE, cumulative = TRUE) +   ggplot2::geom_text(nudge_y = 3) + # Add labels   ggplot2::theme_bw() # Change theme  ## Plot variables contributions to the definition of the first component plot_contributions(X, margin = 2, axes = 1) +   ggplot2::geom_text(nudge_y = 2) + # Add labels   ggplot2::theme_bw() + # Change theme   ggplot2::theme( # Edit theme     # Rotate x axis labels     axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1)   )  ## Plot cos2 plot_cos2(X, margin = 2, axes = c(1, 2)) +   ggplot2::geom_text(nudge_y = 0.05) + # Add labels   ggplot2::theme_bw() + # Change theme   ggplot2::theme( # Edit theme     # Rotate x axis labels     axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1)   )

title: Manual
text: Event dates estimated fitting Gaussian multiple linear regression model factors resulting correspondence analysis - somewhat similar idea introduced Poblome Groenen (2003). model results known dates selection reliable contexts allows predict event dates remaining assemblages. First, correspondence analysis (CA) carried summarize information count matrix \(X\). correspondence analysis \(X\) provides coordinates \(m\) rows along \(q\) factorial components, denoted \(f_{ik} ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], k \\left[ 1,q \right]\). , assuming \(n\) assemblages reliably dated another source, Gaussian multiple linear regression model fitted factorial components \(n\) dated assemblages: \[ t^E_i = \beta_{0} + \sum_{k = 1}^{q} \beta_{k} f_{ik} + \epsilon_i ~\forall \[1,n] \] \(t^E_i\) known date point estimate \(\)th assemblage, \(\beta_k\) regression coefficients \(\epsilon_i\) normally, identically independently distributed random variables, \(\epsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)\). \(n\) equations stacked together written matrix notation \[ t^E = F \beta + \epsilon \] \(\epsilon \sim \mathcal{N}_{n}(0,\sigma^2 I_{n})\), \(\beta = \left[ \beta_0 \cdots \beta_q \right]' \\mathbb{R}^{q+1}\) \[ F =  \begin{bmatrix} 1 & f_{11} & \cdots & f_{1q} \\ 1 & f_{21} & \cdots & f_{2q} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 1 & f_{n1} & \cdots & f_{nq} \end{bmatrix} \] Assuming \(F'F\) nonsingular, ordinary least squares estimator unknown parameter vector \(\beta\) : \[ \widehat{\beta} = \left( F'F \right)^{-1} F' t^E \] Finally, given vector CA coordinates \(f_i\), predicted event date assemblage \(t^E_i\) : \[ \widehat{t^E_i} = f_i \hat{\beta} \] endpoints \(100(1 − \alpha)\)% associated prediction confidence interval given : \[ \widehat{t^E_i} \pm t_{\alpha/2,n-q-1} \sqrt{\widehat{V}} \] \(\widehat{V_i}\) estimator variance prediction error:  \[ \widehat{V_i} = \widehat{\sigma}^2 \left( f_i^T \left( F'F \right)^{-1} f_i + 1 \right) \] \(\widehat{\sigma} = \frac{\sum_{=1}^{n} \left( t_i - \widehat{t^E_i} \right)^2}{n - q - 1}\). probability density event date \(t^E_i\) can described normal distribution: \[ t^E_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\widehat{t^E_i},\widehat{V_i}) \]

text: kairos supports dates expressed CE years (BCE years must given negative numbers). results rounded zero decimal places (sub-annual precision make sense situations). can change behavior options(kairos.precision = x) (x decimal places). kairos uses set S4 classes represent different special types matrix. Please refer documentation arkhe package classes defined. assumes keep data tidy: variable (type/taxa) must saved column observation (sample/case) must saved row.
code: ## Load packages library(kairos) library(folio) # datasets ## Aoristic Analysis data("zuni", package = "folio")  ## Set the start and end dates for each ceramic type dates <- list(   LINO = c(600, 875), KIAT = c(850, 950), RED = c(900, 1050),   GALL = c(1025, 1125), ESC = c(1050, 1150), PUBW = c(1050, 1150),   RES = c(1000, 1200), TULA = c(1175, 1300), PINE = c(1275, 1350),   PUBR = c(1000, 1200), WING = c(1100, 1200), WIPO = c(1125, 1225),   SJ = c(1200, 1300), LSJ = c(1250, 1300), SPR = c(1250, 1300),   PINER = c(1275, 1325), HESH = c(1275, 1450), KWAK = c(1275, 1450) )  ## Keep only assemblages that have a sample size of at least 10 keep <- apply(X = zuni, MARGIN = 1, FUN = function(x) sum(x) >= 10)  ## Calculate date ranges for each assemblage span <- apply(   X = zuni[keep, ],   FUN = function(x, dates) range(unlist(dates[x > 0])),   MARGIN = 1,   dates = dates )  ## Coerce to data.frame span <- as.data.frame(t(span)) colnames(span) <- c("from", "to")  ## Calculate aoristic sum (weights) aorist_weigth <- aoristic(span, step = 50, weight = TRUE) plot(aorist_weigth) ## Rate of change set.seed(12345) aorist_roc <- roc(aorist_weigth, n = 30) plot(aorist_roc)

Colour-blind people difficulty distinguishing colours rainbow. use avoided, Paul Tol’s technical note provides two colour schemes reasonably clear colour-blind vision. remain colour-blind safe, two schemes must comply following conditions: discrete rainbow scheme must interpolated. smooth rainbow scheme used full range (Tol 2021 suggests starting purple).  using smooth rainbow scheme: Start -white instead purple lowest data value occurs often; End red instead brown highest data value occurs often.

title: Color Palette — colour
text: following palettes available. maximum number supported colours brackets, value relevant qualitative colour schemes (divergent sequential schemes linearly interpolated). Qualitative data bright (7), high contrast (3), vibrant (7), muted (9), medium contrast (6), pale (6), dark (6), light (9). Diverging data sunset (11), BuRd (9), PRGn (9). Sequential data YlOrBr (9), iridescent (23), discrete rainbow (23), smooth rainbow (34).

title: Color Palette — colour
text: According Paul Tol's technical note, bright, high contrast, vibrant muted colour schemes colourblind safe. medium contrast colour scheme designed situations needing colour pairs. light colour scheme reasonably distinct normal colourblind vision intended fill labeled cells. pale dark schemes distinct either normal colourblind vision used text background highlight cell table. Refer original document details recommended uses (see references).

title: Color Palette — colour

Colour-blind people difficulty distinguishing colours rainbow. use avoided, Paul Tol's technical note provides two colour schemes reasonably clear colour-blind vision. remain colour-blind safe, two schemes must comply following conditions: discrete rainbow scheme must interpolated. smooth rainbow scheme used full range.

title: Manual
text: arkhe package provides set S4 classes archaeological data matrices. new classes represent different special types matrix. CountMatrix represents absolute frequency data, OccurrenceMatrix represents co-occurrence matrix, CompositionMatrix represents relative frequency data, IncidenceMatrix represents presence/absence data, StratigraphicMatrix represents stratigraphic relationships. assumes keep data tidy: variable (taxon/type) must saved column observation (assemblage/sample) must saved row. Note missing values allowed. internal structure S4 classes implemented arkhe depicted UML class diagram following figure. UML class diagram S4 classes structure.

title: Manual
text: denote \(m \times p\) count matrix \(= \left[ a_{ij} \right] ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) row column sums: \[\begin{align}  a_{\cdot} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} a_{ij} &&  a_{\cdot j} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} a_{ij} &&  a_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} \sum_{j = 1}^{p} a_{ij} &&  \forall a_{ij} \\mathbb{N} \end{align}\]

title: Manual
text: frequency matrix represents relative abundances. denote \(m \times p\) frequency matrix \(B = \left[ b_{ij} \right] ~\forall \\left[ 1,m \right], j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) row column sums: \[\begin{align}  b_{\cdot} = \sum_{j = 1}^{p} b_{ij} = 1 &&  b_{\cdot j} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} b_{ij} &&  b_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{m} \sum_{j = 1}^{p} b_{ij} &&  \forall b_{ij} \\left[ 0,1 \right] \end{align}\]

text: co-occurrence matrix symmetric matrix zeros main diagonal, works many times pairs taxa occur together least one sample. \(p \times p\) co-occurrence matrix \(D = \left[ d_{,j} \right] ~\forall ,j \\left[ 1,p \right]\) defined \(m \times p\) abundance matrix \(= \left[ a_{x,y} \right] ~\forall x \\left[ 1,m \right], y \\left[ 1,p \right]\) : \[ d_{,j} = \sum_{x = 1}^{m} \bigcap_{y = }^{j} a_{xy} \] row column sums: \[\begin{align}   d_{\cdot} = \sum_{j \geqslant }^{p} d_{ij} &&   d_{\cdot j} = \sum_{\leqslant j}^{p} d_{ij} &&   d_{\cdot \cdot} = \sum_{= 1}^{p} \sum_{j \geqslant }^{p} d_{ij} &&   \forall d_{ij} \\mathbb{N} \end{align}\]

title: Representation of Archaeological Data
text: collection classes represent archaeological data. package provides set S4 classes represent different special types matrix (absolute/relative frequency, presence/absence data, co-occurrence matrix, etc.) upon package developers can build subclasses. also provides set generic methods (mutators coercion mechanisms) functions (e.g. predicates). addition, classes general interest (e.g. represent stratigraphic relationships) implemented.

title: Representation of Archaeological Data
text: can install released version arkhe CRAN : development version GitHub :
code: install.packages("arkhe") # install.packages("remotes") remotes::install_github("tesselle/arkhe")

title: Representation of Archaeological Data
text: arkhe provides set S4 classes represent different special types matrix. CountMatrix represents absolute frequency data, OccurrenceMatrix represents co-occurrence matrix, CompositionMatrix represents relative frequency (compositional) data, IncidenceMatrix represents presence/absence data, StratigraphicMatrix represents stratigraphic relationships. assumes keep data tidy: variable (type/taxa) must saved column observation (assemblage/sample) must saved row. new classes simple use inherit base matrix: arkhe uses coercing mechanisms (validation methods) data type conversions: CountMatrix, CompositionMatrix IncidenceMatrix classes special slots: samples replicated measurements/observation, groups group data site/area, dates specify date point estimate assemblage, tqp taq specify chronology assemblage. way chronological information handled somewhat opinionated. Sub-annual precision overkill/meaningless situations: dates assumed expressed years CE stored integers (values coerced .integer() hence truncated towards zero). coercing data.frame *Matrix object, attempt made automatically assign values slots mapping column names. behavior can disabled setting options(arkhe.autodetect = FALSE).
code: ## Load the package library(arkhe) ## Define a count data matrix ## (data will be rounded to zero decimal places, then coerced with as.integer) quanti <- CountMatrix(data = sample(0:10, 100, TRUE), nrow = 10, ncol = 10)  ## Define a logical matrix ## (data will be coerced with as.logical) quali <- IncidenceMatrix(data = sample(0:1, 100, TRUE), nrow = 10, ncol = 10) ## Create a count matrix X <- matrix(data = sample(0:10, 75, TRUE), nrow = 15, ncol = 5)  ## Coerce to absolute frequencies A1 <- as_count(X)  ## Coerce to relative frequencies B <- as_composition(A1)  ## Row sums are internally stored before coercing to a frequency matrix ## (use get_totals() to get these values) ## This allows to restore the source data A2 <- as_count(B) all(A1 == A2) #> [1] TRUE  ## Coerce to presence/absence D <- as_incidence(A1)  ## Coerce to a co-occurrence matrix E <- as_occurrence(A1) Y <- as.data.frame(X) Y$samples <- sample(c("a", "b", "c", "d", "e"), 15, TRUE) Y$groups <- sample(c("A", "B", "C", "D", "E"), 15, TRUE) Y$dates <- sample(1400:1451, 15, TRUE) Y$tpq <- sample(1301:1400, 15, TRUE) # TPQ Y$taq <- sample(1451:1500, 15, TRUE) # TAQ  ## Coerce to a count matrix Z <- as_count(Y)  ## Get groups get_samples(Z) #>  [1] "d" "e" "a" "c" "b" "e" "e" "b" "a" "b" "c" "b" "a" "a" "d"  ## Get groups get_groups(Z) #>  [1] "A" "D" "A" "E" "D" "C" "C" "A" "C" "B" "C" "B" "D" "D" "A"  ## Get dates get_dates(Z) #>  [1] 1428 1400 1430 1413 1421 1421 1451 1418 1442 1413 1401 1435 1447 1412 1405  ## Get chronology get_terminus(Z) #>        tpq  taq #> row1  1382 1497 #> row2  1332 1456 #> row3  1322 1468 #> row4  1373 1490 #> row5  1383 1480 #> row6  1352 1490 #> row7  1329 1499 #> row8  1374 1487 #> row9  1357 1486 #> row10 1398 1495 #> row11 1348 1467 #> row12 1338 1463 #> row13 1354 1477 #> row14 1306 1470 #> row15 1361 1486  ## Summarize summary(Z) #> <AbundanceSummary: 15 x 5> #> A ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #> * Observations: 4 #> * Variables: 5 #> * Replicated measurements: TRUE #> * Chronology: 1322 - 1497 (year CE) #> B ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #> * Observations: 2 #> * Variables: 5 #> * Replicated measurements: TRUE #> * Chronology: 1338 - 1495 (year CE) #> C ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #> * Observations: 4 #> * Variables: 5 #> * Replicated measurements: TRUE #> * Chronology: 1329 - 1499 (year CE) #> D ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #> * Observations: 4 #> * Variables: 5 #> * Replicated measurements: TRUE #> * Chronology: 1306 - 1480 (year CE) #> E ------------------------------------------------------------------------------ #> * Observation: 1 #> * Variables: 5 #> * Replicated measurements: FALSE #> * Chronology: 1373 - 1490 (year CE) #> --------------------------------------------------------------------------------
